cards.text: A {{c1::conditional logic error}} occurs when the programmer incorrectly codes the logic of a conditional statement or loop condition.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code if the intent is to print only when x is greater than 5?
cards.code:
if (x >= 5)
    std::cout << x << " is greater than 5\n";
cards.answer: Should use > instead of >=. The current code will also print when x equals 5.
cards.explanation: Using >= will print when x is 5, but the message says 'greater than 5'. This is a conditional logic error - using the wrong comparison operator.

cards.question: Why does this loop run infinitely?
cards.answer: The loop never increments count, so count <= 10 is always true.
cards.explanation: This is an infinite loop because count is never modified inside the loop body. The condition count <= 10 will never become false.

cards.question: Why does this countdown loop never terminate?
cards.answer: count >= 0 can never be false when count is unsigned. After reaching 0 and decrementing, it wraps to 4294967295.
cards.explanation: Unsigned integers cannot be negative. When count reaches 0 and is decremented, it wraps around to the maximum unsigned value, so count >= 0 is always true.

cards.text: An {{c1::off-by-one error}} is an error that occurs when a loop executes {{c2::one too many}} or {{c3::one too few}} times.

cards.question: What does this code print (intended to print 1 2 3 4 5)?
cards.explanation: This is an off-by-one error. The loop uses < instead of <=, so it stops one iteration early, printing only 1 through 4.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: Operator precedence error: ! has higher precedence than >, so this evaluates as (!x) > y, which is (0) > 7, which is false. The else branch executes.

cards.front: Why is using operator== problematic with floating point numbers?
cards.back: Floating point numbers accumulate small rounding errors from arithmetic operations, so values that should be equal mathematically may not be exactly equal in memory.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: This performs integer division because both operands are integers. The fractional part is dropped. To get floating point division, at least one operand must be a floating point type (e.g., use static_cast).

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Accidental null statement - the semicolon after the if creates an empty statement, so blowUpWorld() always executes.
cards.explanation: The semicolon creates a null statement that is the body of the if. The blowUpWorld() call is not part of the if statement and always executes.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Missing compound statement (braces). blowUpWorld() always executes because it's not inside the if statement.
cards.explanation: Without braces, only the first statement after the if is conditional. The blowUpWorld() call is not part of the if block and always executes.

cards.question: What's wrong with this conditional?
cards.answer: Uses assignment (=) instead of equality (==). This assigns 'y' to c, then evaluates to true, always calling blowUpWorld().
cards.explanation: The assignment operator returns its left operand. c = 'y' assigns 'y' to c and returns c. 'y' (non-zero) converts to bool true, so the if always executes.

cards.question: What does this code most likely print?
cards.explanation: Missing function call operator (). getValue without () gives a function pointer that converts to bool true (which prints as 1), instead of calling the function and printing 5.

cards.text: To fix "forgetting the function call operator" error, change {{c1::std::cout << getValue}} to {{c2::std::cout << getValue()}}.

cards.concept: Most common types of semantic errors in C++
cards.explanation:
The most common semantic errors include:
  • Conditional logic errors (wrong operator: >= vs >, > vs <)
  • Infinite loops (forgetting to update loop variable, unsigned wraparound)
  • Off-by-one errors (using < instead of <=)
  • Incorrect operator precedence (! vs >, && vs ||)
  • Precision issues with floating point types
  • Integer division when floating point expected
  • Accidental null statements (semicolon after if)
  • Missing compound statement (braces)
  • Assignment (=) instead of equality (==) in conditionals
  • Forgetting function call operator ()
